active_ingredients: Docosanol 10% topical cream (e.g., Abreva) - the only FDA-approved OTC medicine clinically shown to shorten cold-sore healing time
indications_and_usage: Uses: treats cold sores and fever blisters on the face or lips (caused by herpes simplex virus); used at the very first sign (tingling, itching, redness, or bump), it can shorten healing time and the duration of symptoms (tingling, pain, burning, itching). It works by helping block the virus from entering healthy skin cells. It is not a cure for the herpes virus and does not prevent future outbreaks.
contraindications: Do not use if you are allergic to docosanol or any inactive ingredient; do not use inside the mouth, in or near the eyes, or on the genitals.
warnings_and_cautions: For external use only on the lips and face. Apply at the first sign of a cold sore for the best effect. Wash hands before and after applying to avoid spreading the virus to other areas (especially the eyes) or to other people. Use only on cold sores on the lips and face - if a cold sore is inside the mouth, on the genitals, or you have a weakened immune system, see a doctor. Stop use and ask a doctor if your cold sore gets worse or is not healed within 10 days.
adverse_reactions: Headache, application-site reactions (mild burning, itching, dryness); rarely allergic reactions.
dosage_and_administration: Adults and children 12 years and over: wash hands; apply a thin layer to the affected area on the lips or face at the first sign of a cold sore; rub in gently and completely; apply 5 times a day until healed (do not exceed 10 days); wash hands afterward. Do not share the tube. Children under 12: ask a doctor.
overdosage: Topical overdose is not a concern; ingestion of small amounts is not expected to be harmful. Contact Poison Control (1-800-222-1222) for large ingestions.
